name: Brihaspati
description: Brihaspati, also known as Brahmanaspati, is a prominent deity in the Hindu pantheon, revered as the guru (teacher) of the gods and the embodiment of wisdom, eloquence, and religious merit. He is often associated with the planet Jupiter (Guru Graha) and is considered a benevolent and auspicious figure. Brihaspati is the master of prayers and rituals, believed to possess the power to grant success, prosperity, and spiritual enlightenment. His influence extends to areas such as law, ethics, and diplomacy, making him a crucial figure for rulers and scholars alike. He is depicted as a wise and learned sage, often adorned in yellow robes and holding prayer beads or a staff, symbolizing his authority and knowledge. He represents the power of intellect, reason, and the pursuit of righteous conduct within the cosmic order.
subtitle: Guru of the Gods

keyMyths.title: Tara's Abduction
keyMyths.description: One of the most famous myths involving Brihaspati concerns his wife, Tara, who was abducted by Chandra, the moon god. This led to a fierce war between the gods and the asuras, known as the Tarakamaya War. Eventually, Brahma intervened and forced Chandra to return Tara to Brihaspati. However, Tara was pregnant with Chandra's child, Budha, who later became the god of Mercury. This tale highlights Brihaspati's role in maintaining cosmic order and the consequences of transgressions.

keyMyths.title: Brihaspati's Teachings
keyMyths.description: Brihaspati is renowned for his profound teachings and guidance to the gods. He is considered the source of all Vedic knowledge and is often consulted by Indra and other deities for advice on matters of state and warfare. His counsel is always rooted in dharma (righteousness) and aims to uphold the cosmic balance. He is said to have revealed the sacred mantras and rituals that enable the gods to maintain their power and influence over the universe.

keyMyths.title: Brihaspati and the Asuras
keyMyths.description: In some accounts, Brihaspati is also depicted as having a complex relationship with the asuras. He is sometimes portrayed as having tricked the asuras or providing them with deceptive teachings to weaken them, thereby aiding the gods in their battles. This highlights the strategic and intellectual prowess of Brihaspati, showcasing his ability to manipulate situations to achieve the desired outcome, even if it involves deception.
